author: Allan Watkins
content: Great post. Ref lock-in, I wonder if there is an historical aspect around peoples’ experience with software vendors gaining dominance, customers finding it difficult to move away and the vendor finding they can increase prices without losing too many customers. Without mentioning any names, there’s a database vendor that crops up now and again… All the cloud providers are in growth mode – fighting for market share (perhaps above making money this year) and when the market stabilises (and perhaps they have some customers locked in) they can start looking to maximise profits. I think maybe people remember being burnt on lock-in in the on-prem world and want to learn from the experience. I think you are right though – I think there are some valid concerns around going all in with one provider, but for the majority I can’t see that the effort/expense of multi-cloud makes it a better option.

author: Ian Miell
content: This post is gold. Thanks for writing.

author: Dimitris Moraitis
content:
Multi-Cloud can be hard to do right and there are plenty of traps along the way. It sure doesn’t make sense to go that way just for Disaster Recovery, unless you need to be prepared for the unlikely event that Amazon or Google will suddenly get wiped out of the planet.

That said, going Multi-Cloud is indeed unavoidable in a growing number of settings. So, instead of looking at it as a source of troubles, it can be leveraged as a way to extract the best features out of each provider, to avoid lock-in wherever it makes business sense and to minimize costs by distributing workloads accordingly. That introduces new issues regarding access control, cost analysis, auditing and governance which are best managed by a Multi-Cloud Management Platform.

If you’re looking for such a tool check out mist.io

It’s an open source CMP that supports most popular public & private clouds, as well as Hypervisors and container hosts. It takes care of provisioning, monitoring, RBAC, cost analysis and automation/orchestration. It can also be used to deploy Kubernetes clusters on any supported cloud.

Disclaimer: I’m one of the founders.

author: Dario
content:
Interesting take on the “new Gospel” of multi-cloud… that said, I do firmly believe that working with abstraction layers (virtualization, Cloud, containers, etc…) requires building services with a model-driven approach for ease of re-use and maintenance!

Disclaimer: I work at Puppet ;-)

author: Jan Penninkhof
content:
Interesting post. Thanks so much for writing this.

What would you think of a PaaS on to of a MCaaS (multi-cloud as a service, just thought of that while I was reading your story). At least it wouldn’t be dumping the burden of multi-cloud in the desk of an operations team like you mentioned, but does provide you with the benefits such as no lock-in an a broader regional coverage (the well-known US hyper scale cloud provides aren’t doing so well in eg China).

At this moment SAP is the only company that provides this kind of service. And It makes me proud to work with a company that has this visionary approach. (Disclaimer applies, writing this on a personal note though ;)

I do believe that with containerization such as Kubernetes and Cloud foundry, the difficulties in setting up multi-cloud infra is eventually getting sorted. There are now even very nice open source initiatives that address managing kubernetes cluster in several cloud providers as if they were one. And what to think of FaaS. Once that’s more commoditized and standardized it will also work as a kathalyst for multi cloud.

Anyway, gave me good food for thought on this Sunday morning. Thanks again for writing this fantasy piece.

children.author: Jan Penninkhof
children.content: Sorry, meant fantastic. Was writing that on my mobile… I’m afraid autocorrect did that..

author: Rakhi Makad
content: Fantastic read! Thank you for this. Question – what about platform providers? They are usually pushed to provide multi-cloud options by enterprises – the retail and AWS example quoted at the begin is quite prevalent, but the list keeps passively growing with automotive and GCP, Pharma and AWS… Any thoughts?

author: Anthony T
content:
“Either the abstraction reduces things to a common interface or it doesn’t. If it does, it’s unclear how it can leverage differentiated provider features and remain cloud-agnostic. If it doesn’t, it’s unclear what the value of it is or how it can be truly multi-cloud.”

Isn’t this what the Open Service Broker API is for?

https://thenewstack.io/using-cloud-foundry-or-kubernetes-get-to-know-the-open-service-broker/

author: Rahul
content: You have left out another (One of the most – aside from best fit) valid reason for multi-cloud and that is for compliance and risk mitigation reasons. Ofcourse, you need to be able to invest on abstractions and provide underlying cloud services as a service to the business logic abstracting the native features. Its all about the fundamentals of software architecture and engineering practices. Its required in the on-prem, required in one cloud or multiple clouds if you want to be agile, portable and risk averse.
